payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
A music supervisor at an ad agency has six tracks to clear by Friday. Each needs writer splits confirmed, master rights confirmed, sample clearances surfaced, and the new question that's been added in the last 18 months: is any of this AI? Some have publishing splits that don't match what the distributor reported. One has a co-writer who isn't responding. One has a sample whose source nobody documented. One has metadata that says human but no chain to back it up.

That's a typical Friday. The supervisor hires legal review for each ambiguous track. The label or library spends senior staff time chasing splits. The agency builds two weeks of buffer into the timeline to absorb it. Everyone budgets for friction nobody calls friction.

This is a CFO problem. Nobody in the chain has named it that way.

What it actually costs

Sync clearance is one of several recurring cost surfaces in music finance that share a common cause. The chain of authorship for a piece of music isn't reliably verifiable, and every cost surface downstream of that fact is paying for the uncertainty.

Legal review time on sync clearance, sample clearance, and split disputes. False-positive enforcement when an artist's account gets flagged wrongly and lawyers up. Detection spend that scales with content volume rather than with revenue. Catalog contamination as AI tracks slip past confidence thresholds and accumulate streams that have to be retroactively unwound. Black-box royalties that the system collected but couldn't distribute because the rights holder couldn't be identified. Dispute legal spend on royalty arbitration where the underlying claim is probabilistic and therefore disputable forever.

None of those is labeled "the cost of unsigned music" on a P&L. They sit under legal, ops, moderation, content review, and financial reserves. The single architectural cause is invisible to the accounting layer because no system was built to surface it.

SIX COST LINES, ONE CAUSE

Sync clearance legal review
False-positive enforcement
Detection ops spend
Catalog contamination
Black-box royalties
Dispute legal spend

Unverified authorship

One architectural cause. Provenance changes the cause.

Why sync is the cleanest example

Sync is where the cost shows up first because sync has the shortest tolerance for ambiguity. A streaming royalty dispute can take 18 months to settle. A sync clearance has to close in two weeks. The cost of ambiguity compresses into a small window, with multiple parties on a deadline, each carrying liability.

When provenance is verified at the source, sync clearance stops being an investigation. What the track is, who wrote it, and whether it is AI are already established at creation, so confirming them is a matter of checking signatures rather than reconstructing a history.

The splits were signed at the session. The masters carry their own signatures. AI status resolves to yes or no instead of a confidence score, and the sample chain can be walked end to end.

Production music libraries feel this acutely. The volume of AI-generated tracks now flooding sync libraries is changing the economics of cataloging itself. Libraries that don't sort signed from unsigned music will absorb compounding clearance risk for every track they ship to a buyer.

What changes with provenance

The structural argument is the same one that ends every other cost surface on the list. Verification at creation is one-time. Every downstream check is a hash comparison. The cost of verifying that a track is what it says it is becomes a fixed cost, paid once, and amortized across every downstream use.

Sync clearance closes faster. Splits don't get disputed because the splits were signed at the session. False-positive enforcement drops because there's a verifiable signal that doesn't need a classifier. Detection spend declines because verification doesn't need detection. Black-box royalties shrink because the matching at collection is deterministic, not forensic.

None of this happens overnight. None of it requires the entire industry to flip on one day. It requires a CFO at a label, a distributor, or a rights holder to look at the cost lines and ask whether the architectural cause is worth solving once instead of paying for forever.

The balance-sheet question

Detection-first approaches are an operating cost. Provenance is capital expenditure. The economic question every rights holder faces is whether to keep funding the operating cost of unsigned music indefinitely, or to fund the one-time cost of building verification into the workflow.

Detection's costs compound. Provenance's costs amortize. Those are different lines on the same income statement, with different long-run trajectories.

Provenance is a CFO problem. The technology decision is downstream of the financial one.
payload.title: Provenance as a CFO problem
